summary: Dan Shapiro announces he has switched his blog's RSS syndication to Feedburner's smartfeed system. He explains the change at escalating levels of technical detail, from 'nothing changed' for casual readers to redirect specifics for RSS power users. The post is a practical tech update wrapped in humor, advising readers to point their newsreaders at his canonical URL rather than the Feedburner URL directly, so he can revert easily if needed. It's a snapshot of mid-2000s blogging infrastructure concerns.
key_points: Shapiro migrated his blog syndication to Feedburner's smartfeed, which auto-converts between RSS, Atom, and other formats
He structures the explanation in escalating 'geekiness levels' so readers can stop when it stops being relevant to them
He advises readers to use his canonical URL rather than the Feedburner URL directly, preserving his ability to switch providers without breaking feeds
The move was partly motivated by wanting aggregated reader statistics to track his audience size
money_quotes: Read until it stops making sense or you stop caring.
I don't get Google ad revenue since you're not visiting my page, but that's OK, 'cause you're my BOY and I LOVE you.
It also lets me aggregate my site statistics so I can see how many readers I have on my feed, thereby feeding my monstrous, out-of-control ego.
Sometimes you just need to give your brain a break. No, I'm not still on vicodin.
key_insight: A lighthearted 2004 blog housekeeping post about RSS feed migration that captures the era's blogging culture and infrastructure concerns.
